title: Voice loop end-to-end: make listen -> respond -> speak demoable behind one 'voice on' toggle, or document the exact blockers
current_state: Pieces exist but the loop is unproven as one flow: speak (pyttsx3 offline TTS) works; listen/transcribe_file (Groq Whisper) require audio hardware and are rarely exercised; wake-word detection (tools/tools_wakeword.py) is dormant by default; agent/voice_loop.py exists but the full listen -> respond -> speak cycle has no test and no documented invocation path. Phase 8 roadmap item (PI.md section 12); no hackathon track needs it - filed so it is not lost.
target_state: Either (a) 'voice on' in the REPL starts a working desktop loop: mic capture -> Groq Whisper -> normal turn -> pyttsx3 speech, exiting cleanly on 'voice off'; or (b) if hardware/platform genuinely blocks it, a short honest write-up in docs/ naming each blocker and the smallest unblock - so the next attempt starts from facts instead of archaeology.
migration_plan: 1. Read agent/voice_loop.py end to end; trace what it actually wires today and where it is invoked (or not) from pi_agent.py.
2. Attempt the loop live on the dev box with real mic + speakers; note every failure with its traceback.
3. Fix what is small (device selection, sample rate, toggle wiring); anything structural becomes its own follow-up ticket.
4. Test: the loop's glue logic with fake STT/TTS callables (no audio hardware in tests - stays non-costly); assert 'voice on' enters and 'voice off' exits the loop cleanly.
5. python scripts/verify.py PASS. Wake word stays dormant - explicitly out of scope here.
risk_notes: Audio on Windows is fiddly (device indices, pyttsx3 SAPI quirks); timebox the live-debugging to a session. Outcome (b) - an honest blocker doc - is a fully legitimate close, not a failure.
